title: Why Learning Rate Schedules Exist
concept: One fixed learning rate is a compromise: it may help a network move quickly early on, but later training often needs smaller, calmer updates to refine what the model has found.

exercises.prompt: Recap: If a loss curve keeps bouncing back and forth instead of settling, which training setting is a common suspect?

options.text: A learning rate that is too high.
Too many emojis in the lesson title.
A dataset having labels at all.

exercises.explain: Right. A learning rate that is too large can make training zigzag or overshoot instead of settling down.

cards.title: Highway speed, parking-lot speed
cards.body: If you drove at parking-lot speed on the highway, the trip would take forever. But if you drove at highway speed while squeezing into a parking space, you would overshoot instantly. Training a deep network has the same issue: early progress and late fine-tuning often need different step sizes.

cards.title: Why schedules exist
cards.body: A learning rate schedule changes the learning rate as training continues. Early on, larger steps can help the model escape obviously bad regions and learn fast. Later, smaller steps help it make careful adjustments instead of bouncing around a good solution.

cards.title: Big strides, then careful footing
cards.body: When hiking down a wide trail, you can take long strides. Near a narrow rocky ledge, you shorten your steps so you do not slip past the safe path. A schedule gives training that same change in rhythm.

cards.title: Fast start, messy finish
cards.body: Imagine training an image classifier. A fixed learning rate might drop the loss quickly in the first few epochs, then keep knocking the weights past a good valley instead of letting them settle. Lowering the rate later can turn that noisy wobble into steadier refinement.

cards.title: One good rate is not always good forever
cards.body: A common myth is that if one learning rate works at the start, it should stay unchanged until the end. In reality, training is not one stage. The model often needs bold moves while it is still lost and gentler moves once it is close.

cards.title: One gear vs. changing gears
cards.body: A fixed rate is simple, but long training often benefits from shifting gears over time.

left.text:
Same step size at the start.
Same step size near the finish.
Simple, but sometimes too blunt.

right.text:
Bigger steps when exploring.
Smaller steps when refining.
Better matched to long training.

exercises.prompt: Why is one fixed learning rate often too crude for long training runs?

options.text: Because early training and late training often need different step sizes.
Because fixed rates prevent backpropagation from existing.
Because any fixed rate automatically deletes the validation set.
Because schedules change the model architecture each epoch.

exercises.explain: Exactly. A single rate is a compromise, but the training job usually changes over time.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Later in training, a deep network often benefits from smaller updates than it needed at the very beginning.
exercises.explain: True. Once the model is near a useful solution, smaller corrections often help it settle instead of overshooting.

exercises.sentence: A learning rate schedule helps a model take bigger steps early and ___ steps later.
exercises.explain: Correct. Schedules often keep the early speed but reduce the step size for later refinement.

exercises.prompt: Which situation best shows why a schedule can help?

options.text: The loss falls quickly at first, then keeps wobbling around a good value instead of settling.
The model trains for one mini-batch and is already perfect forever.
The network changes from an image model into a text model halfway through training.

exercises.explain: Right. That pattern often means the model still has enough power to move, but now needs gentler steps.

exercises.prompt: A speech model learns quickly for the first part of training, but near a good solution the loss keeps bouncing above and below the same value instead of settling. What is the best explanation for why a learning rate schedule could help?

options.text: The model may need large steps early and smaller steps later, so one fixed rate is too blunt for the whole run.
Schedules work by turning labels into hidden neurons.
A schedule removes the need for gradients entirely.
The only possible problem is that the network has too few output classes.

exercises.explain: Exactly. The schedule is useful because the training job changes over time: explore first, refine later.
